figures.caption: Figure 2. Change of tr(FF − I) (a)before and (b)after adding the diffusive F term in new model. We observe more stable behavior of F near the interfacial region.

Figure 9. Case C ′ in (a) and (b) and Case C in (c) and (d) in Table 1. First column plots are AA-PINN training points sampling and Second column plots are ϕ profile as time changes. Initial condition for ϕ profile is given as two thrombi as shown at t = 0 in phi profile (b) and (d). As in the more clear gathering of clots in (d) without boundary diffusive part which is shown in (b), λγ of (d) is bigger than the one of (b), while fixing the λ in both cases. Hence, λγ drives the dissipative energy to decrease |ϕ(1 − ϕ)|2 more while separating phase-field.
Figure 11. Axial section figures of Case D in Table 1. (a) does not use AA-PINN energyadaptive sampling and (b) uses it.
Figure 10. Case C ′ in (a) and (b) of Table 1.

Figure 12. h = 0.05 of Case D′ in Table 1. (a) is the residual loss graph and (b) is the mass conservation error and divergence free error. The loss and and errors are worse than h = 0.08 case in Figure 6. We observe that larger values of h are numerically easier to simulate.

Figure 5. Axial section figures of evolution for each unit train time interval ∆unitt = 0.05(a) and ∆unitt = 1.0(b). These have parameter as in the Case A in Table 1 and the clots are static with small enough change of ϕ value as we intended with the parameter setting. (c) is phi profile for For ∆unitt = 0.05 and (d) is difference for the both cases of ∆unitt = 0.05 and ∆unitt = 0.1. On plot (d), at t = 0.05, 0.15, 0.45, the both cases are in different in the view that one is at the end of the unit train time interval of 0.05(Exp(∆t = 0.05) and another is in the middle in the time interval of 0.1(Exp(∆t = 0.1). The separating interval of transfer learning points as t = 0.15, 0.45 of unit train time interval ∆unitt = 0.05 have small error bound compared with ∆unitt = 0.1 case which does not have transfer learning at the points. This means transfer learning perform well with small error.
Table 1. Parameter settings for each case. For the challenging cases marked (E), PINN solver employed total energy-adaptive sampling from Auto-Adaptive PINN([23]).

Figure 6. Mass conservation error and divergence free condition error for each of unit train time intervals 0.5(a) and 1.0(b). The parameter setting comes from the Case A in Table 1. We can check that the unit train tine interval Experiment of ∆unitt = 0.05 performs better than Experiment of ∆unitt = 0.1 case.
Figure 7. Energy dissipation for each of unit train time intervals 0.05(a) and 0.1(b). The parameter setting comes from the Case A in Table 1.

Figure 1. On time domain [0, .2], phase field variable ϕ (=0 on clot and =1 on blood) on (a)original thrombus system with λe = 1 on thrombus and λe = 0 on blood in [6], (b)modified new model with generalized viscoelasticity ν(ϕ) = (1 −ϕ) + 10−5ϕ. (a) displays mixed and ambiguous phase field shape change, not describing interplay of dynamics on shock region. However, (b) shows more stable formation for the interface change for the ϕ as better performance to catch the quickly changing gradient phenomenon on the shock.
Figure 14. Energy dissipation figures. These have parameter as in the Case D′ in Table 1.
Figure 13. Energy dissipation figures. These have parameter as in the Case A in Table 1 where (a) has ∆unitt = 0.05 and (b) has ∆unitt = 0.1.

Figure 8. Axial section figures of evolutionas (a) and (b). These (a) and (b) have parameter as in the Case B and Case B′ in Table 1 respectively. As in the more diffusive figure as (b), λ of (b) is bigger than the one of (a) twice, while fixing the λγ in the mixed energy. λ drives the dissipative energy to decrease |∇ϕ|2.
Figure 4. (a) Before applying AA-PINN method and (b) after applying this on same experiment setting as Figure 9.
